name: arm-cortex-expert
description: Expert assistance for ARM Cortex-M microcontroller development including bare-metal programming, peripheral configuration, RTOS integration, and debug optimization.
capabilities: Explain ARM Cortex-M architecture, registers, and instruction sets
Guide peripheral configuration including GPIO, timers, UART, SPI, and I2C
Assist with interrupt handling and vector table configuration
Provide debug strategies using JTAG, SWD, and printf debugging
Support RTOS concepts including task scheduling, semaphores, and mutexes
Help optimize code for MCU resource constraints
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a prompt-only skill providing ARM Cortex-M development assistance. Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 issues detected. No executable code, no network access, no filesystem operations. The skill only generates text responses about embedded development topics. Risk score: 0/100.
valueStatement: ARM Cortex-M developers need expert guidance on embedded programming patterns, peripheral configuration, and RTOS implementation. This skill provides detailed assistance for bare-metal and RTOS-based firmware development.
limitations: Cannot compile or flash code to physical hardware
Does not provide simulated hardware execution
Limited to general guidance, not project-specific implementations

useCases.title: Learn ARM Cortex-M Fundamentals
useCases.description: New embedded developers can ask questions about Cortex-M architecture, register layouts, and programming concepts to build foundational knowledge.

useCases.title: Debug Firmware Issues
useCases.description: Developers stuck on hardware or software bugs can get expert guidance on debugging strategies, common pitfalls, and diagnostic techniques.

useCases.title: Design RTOS-Based Systems
useCases.description: Engineers implementing FreeRTOS or other RTOS can get help with task design, synchronization primitives, and memory management.

promptTemplates.title: Architecture Question
promptTemplates.prompt: Explain how [specific Cortex-M feature, e.g., NVIC interrupt prioritization] works in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.
promptTemplates.scenario: Learning about specific Cortex-M architecture features

promptTemplates.title: Peripheral Configuration
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I configure [peripheral name, e.g., UART] for [baud rate, e.g., 115200] communication on [MCU name, e.g., STM32F4]?

promptTemplates.title: Interrupt Handling
promptTemplates.prompt: Write example code for handling [interrupt type] interrupts on ARM Cortex-M, including vector table configuration.

promptTemplates.title: RTOS Task Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Design an RTOS task structure for [application, e.g., data acquisition] including task priorities, stack sizing, and inter-task communication.

bestPractices: Start with the reference manual and errata sheet for your specific MCU before writing code
Use CMSIS-compliant device headers for portable, maintainable code
Initialize system clocks before configuring peripherals to avoid unexpected behavior
antiPatterns: Do not access registers without checking the clock is enabled first
Avoid busy-wait loops for timing in production code
Do not disable interrupts for extended periods - use critical sections wisely

faq.question: Which Cortex-M core should I choose for my project?
faq.answer: Choose M0+ for lowest power, M3 for general purpose, M4/M7 for DSP or math-heavy applications. Consider available peripherals and memory size as well.

faq.question: How do I debug Cortex-M firmware?
faq.answer: Use SWD or JTAG debugger with tools like OpenOCD, ST-Link, or J-Link. Set breakpoints, watch variables, and use printf debugging via ITM or UART.

faq.question: What is CMSIS and why should I use it?
faq.answer: CMSIS is the Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard. It provides consistent device headers, core access functions, and DSP libraries across ARM Cortex chips.

faq.question: When should I use an RTOS vs bare-metal?
faq.answer: Use bare-metal for simple applications with linear execution. Use RTOS when you have multiple concurrent tasks, need real-time scheduling, or complex inter-task communication.

faq.question: How do I handle critical sections in Cortex-M?
faq.answer: Use CPSID/CPSIE instructions to disable/enable interrupts globally, or use BASEPRI register to mask interrupts above a certain priority level.

faq.question: What tools do I need for Cortex-M development?
faq.answer: You need a toolchain (GCC ARM Embedded, Keil, or IAR), a debugger (ST-Link, J-Link, or CMSIS-DAP), and ideally an IDE like VS Code with appropriate extensions.
